target.title: [codex] active-memory default to configured agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Default Active Memory to every configured OpenClaw agent when `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` is omitted or empty.
- Preserve the existing explicit allowlist behavior when `config.agents` is set.
- Add regression coverage for both default-all and explicit-allowlist normalization.

This matters because today omitting `config.agents` normalizes to an empty list, which causes Active Memory to be disabled for every agent. The intended outcome is that operators can enable Active Memory across current and future configured agents without maintaining a duplicated allowlist.

Out of scope: changing chat type filtering, per-session toggles, memory indexing behavior, or the memory tools used by the Active Memory sub-agent.

Review focus: whether the fallback to `agents.list[].id` is the right default and whether explicit `config.agents` remains unchanged.

## Linked context

Closes #

Related #

This came from running OpenClaw with multiple Telegram topics routed to separate agents and needing Active Memory to apply consistently without copying the same agent list into plugin config.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  Omitting `plugins.entries.active-memory.config.agents` currently normalizes to an empty allowlist, so Active Memory skips every agent instead of applying to all configured agents.
- Real environment tested:
  OpenClaw 2026.6.5 on a Linux host with a systemd user gateway, Telegram group topics routed to `main`, `sementes`, `cloudfarm`, and `pedro-agenda`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  Applied the equivalent runtime change locally, removed `active-memory.config.agents`, restarted `openclaw-gateway`, then checked gateway status and memory search.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
openclaw gateway status --deep
Gateway version: 2026.6.5
Runtime: running
Capability: admin-capable
```

```text
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since "3 minutes ago"
[gateway] http server listening (12 plugins: active-memory, brave, browser, canvas, codex, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, openai, phone-control, talk-voice, telegram; 12.1s)
```

```text
openclaw memory search --query "Pedro SeedSearch Markus" --max-results 5
0.545 memory/pendencias-grupo.md:1-21
0.516 memory/2026-03-31.md:1-37
0.453 memory/dreaming/light/2026-06-05.md:43-72
```

- Observed result after fix:
  Active Memory no longer required a hardcoded `config.agents` list to apply across configured agents.
- What was not tested:
  The full `extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts` Vitest run did not complete locally; it remained in build/plugin timing output and was stopped.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  The production server was restored to upstream behavior after proof collection, using an explicit `config.agents` list until this PR is merged upstream.
- Before evidence:
  Reading the current plugin implementation showed `normalizePluginConfig({}).agents` becomes `[]`, while `isEnabledForAgent` requires `config.agents.includes(agentId)`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Regression coverage added:

- `defaults to all configured agents when no active-memory agent allowlist is set`
- `keeps an explicit active-memory agent allowlist`

Known validation limitation:

```text
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts --maxWorkers=1
```

This did not complete in the local environment; it remained in build/plugin timing output and was stopped.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

Highest-risk area:
Operators who intentionally omitted `config.agents` expecting Active Memory to run nowhere would now get Active Memory for all configured agents.

Risk mitigation:
Explicit `config.agents` still behaves as an allowlist. Operators can set `enabled: false`, disable the plugin entry, or provide a narrow `agents` list when they want less coverage.

## Current review state

Next action:
Await maintainer review and CI.

Still waiting on:
Maintainer feedback, CI, and any requested stronger runtime proof.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed:
Filled in real behavior proof after the `triage: needs-real-behavior-proof` label was applied.

target.title: fix(agents): carry provenance through active subagent wakes
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #91356, #91330, #91333.

Carry typed `inputProvenance` through active subagent wake steering so resumed requester turns keep an explicit inter-session provenance signal instead of arriving as untyped queued messages.

This is intentionally narrower than the closed #91333 approach. It does not suppress automatic-mode finals based on ordinary `message(action="send")` receipts, and it does not infer terminal source-reply ownership from `messageId`, target matching, or prose.

## Why

The maintainer feedback on #91330/#91333 clarified the unsafe boundary: a successful send proves delivery, but it does not prove that the send was the terminal source reply. That replacement needs an explicit terminal-source-reply contract and thread identity; this PR does not attempt that.

This patch only handles the active wake side: when a subagent completion wakes an active requester session, the runtime already knows this is an inter-session wake. The queued steering message should carry that typed provenance through the active run / reply-run bridge so downstream routing and final-selection logic can reason from explicit metadata instead of an untyped internal wake.

## Changes

- Add active-session steering options for `inputProvenance`.
- Preserve the existing `AgentSession.steer(text, images)` call shape while allowing `steer(text, { images, inputProvenance })`.
- Apply provenance to queued steering user messages with `applyInputProvenanceToUserMessage`.
- Carry queue options through reply-run fallback queueing without changing the legacy no-options call shape.
- Pass `subagent_announce` / completion provenance through active requester wake queue options.
- Add regression coverage for active wake, retry/best-effort, stale-thread handoff, generated-media wake, and reply-run fallback paths.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: active subagent completion wakes could cross the requester-session boundary as untyped steering messages, even though the runtime already knows the wake is an inter-session `subagent_announce`/completion handoff.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout based on current `origin/main` (`575cae59d4864d9e215ee66d80d26f4e345021b5`), branch `agent/xiaozhua/active-wake-provenance-clean`, using focused Vitest plus changed-file lint/diff gates.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.queue-message.test.ts \
  src/agents/sessions/sdk.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/effective-reply-route.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts

./node_modules/.bin/oxlint \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.queue-message.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.queue-message.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.ts \
  src/agents/sessions/agent-session.ts \
  src/agents/sessions/sdk.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts

git diff --cached --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
Vitest: 3 shards passed; 166 tests passed.
Oxlint: Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
git diff --cached --check: passed.
```

Observed result after fix: active subagent requester wakes now carry explicit `inputProvenance` through active-run and reply-run queue paths into the queued user message. Regression tests assert the provenance is present on normal, retry/best-effort, stale-thread, and generated-media wake paths while preserving legacy no-options queue call shapes.

What was not tested: no live Telegram/Slack/bncr end-to-end run was performed. #91356 was closed as not reproducible in current core, so this PR is framed as route/provenance boundary hardening rather than a claimed live-channel fix.

target.title: fix(whatsapp): confirm auth persistence before login success
target.body:
## Summary

- Wait for the selected WhatsApp auth directory to settle after the socket reports connected.
- Re-read the persisted auth state before returning login success.
- Return a retryable failure when auth is still stabilizing or the linked creds are not on disk yet.
- Keep the change inside the shared login waiter so QR login and recovered-session login use the same success boundary.

## Linked context

Closes #92039

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: WhatsApp login could report success as soon as Baileys opened the socket, before queued credential persistence was confirmed on disk.
- Real environment tested: local checkout on macOS with the WhatsApp extension test shard.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: the focused command passed with 3 files and 42 tests.
- Observed result after fix: the shared login waiter now returns failure for both unstable auth and missing persisted linked auth after socket-open; existing QR login success tests now have to prove the auth state becomes linked.
- What was not tested: I did not run a real WhatsApp QR login or Docker rebuild/restart cycle.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this environment does not have a disposable WhatsApp account available for a live relink/restart proof.
- Before evidence: the old `waitForWhatsAppLoginResult` returned `connected` immediately after `waitForWaConnection` resolved.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/connection-controller.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/login-qr.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Updated coverage in `connection-controller.test.ts` for the two race cases: queued auth still unstable, and socket-open before linked auth is visible on disk. Updated QR login tests so successful paths model auth becoming linked after the connection opens.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `Yes`

Highest-risk area: WhatsApp login may now ask the user to retry instead of reporting success when persistence is delayed or missing.

Mitigation: the check uses the existing `readWebAuthExistsForDecision` helper, which already waits on the per-auth-dir credential save queue and classifies timeout as unstable.

## Current review state

Ready for review. A maintainer with a disposable WhatsApp setup should still run the Docker/restart proof from #92039 before treating this as fully verified in the reported deployment shape.

target.title: fix(model-fallback): don't rethrow provider-side AbortErrors as user cancellations
target.body:
## Problem

When the LLM API closes the connection mid-stream, the fetch layer surfaces `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` with **no external abort signal triggered** (`"externalAbort: false"` in trajectory). The existing guard `shouldRethrowAbort()` returned `false` for these errors (because `isTimeoutError` matched the "operation was aborted" message), so they fell through to the fallback loop but were never retried — the error propagated up and produced `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN` in group/channel sessions, permanently silencing the topic.

## Fix

1. **model-fallback.ts**: Add an explicit guard **before** `coerceToFailoverError` in `runFallbackCandidate`: if the error is an `AbortError` and the external abort signal is already aborted, rethrow immediately (user/gateway cancellation). Provider-side `AbortError`s (no external signal) fall through to the next fallback candidate.

```typescript
// Before coerceToFailoverError normalization:
if (isFallbackAbortError(err) && params.abortSignal?.aborted) {
  throw err;
}
```

2. **compact.ts**: Thread `params.abortSignal` into the `runWithModelFallback` call so compaction cancellations are correctly forwarded to the new guard.

Also removes the unused `shouldRethrowAbort` helper and `isTimeoutError` import.

## Scope

- **3 files changed**, +66 / -8
- `model-fallback.ts`: guard moved before normalization (core fix)
- `compact.ts`: abortSignal forwarding (P2 fix)
- `model-fallback.test.ts`: 3 regression tests

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Provider-side `AbortError("This operation was aborted")` (no external abort signal) was misclassified by `shouldRethrowAbort()` → `isTimeoutError()` matching the message, causing the fallback loop to silently swallow the error. In group/channel sessions this produced `SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`, permanently silencing the topic with zero visible response.

- **Real environment tested**: macOS (Apple Silicon), Node v22, patched OpenClaw dist at `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/model-fallback--w47Y_pS.js`. Gateway restarted with patched dist. Guard logic verified against the actual production module code.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# 1. Patch the dist file with the fix
# 2. Restart the gateway
launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/ai.openclaw.gateway

# 3. Run the proof script against the patched dist
node /tmp/real-behavior-proof-v2.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Patched dist verification ===
File exists: true
Size: 50112 bytes
New guard (isFallbackAbortError && abortSignal.aborted): PRESENT ✓
Old guard (shouldRethrowAbort && !normalizedFailover): REMOVED ✓

=== Guard behavior (patched code) ===
Provider-side AbortError (no signal): FALLTHROUGH (provider abort → retry) ✓
User cancel (AbortError reason):     RETHROW (user cancellation) ✓
User cancel (TimeoutError reason):   RETHROW (user cancellation) ✓

=== Compact abortSignal forwarding ===
compact.ts passes abortSignal to runWithModelFallback: YES ✓

=== RESULT: ALL CORRECT ✅ ===
Provider-side abort → fallback retry (topic recovers)
User cancellation → immediate stop (no wasted provider calls)
Compact caller → abortSignal forwarded (no silent compaction retries)
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Provider-side AbortError (no signal): guard does NOT fire → error enters `coerceToFailoverError` → falls through to next fallback candidate → topic recovers
  - User cancel (AbortError reason): guard fires (`abortSignal.aborted = true`) → rethrows immediately → no wasted provider calls
  - User cancel (TimeoutError reason): `isTerminalAbort` catches first → rethrows immediately
  - Compact caller: `abortSignal` forwarded to `runWithModelFallback` → new guard correctly stops on user cancellation
  - All 79 model-fallback tests pass, including 3 new regression tests

- **What was not tested**: Live gateway end-to-end with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction (guard logic verified against production dist module; full network-layer round-trip not driven). `agent-runner-execution.ts` SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN routing (separate follow-up if needed).

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: fix(skill-workshop): align agent_end hook timeout with max reviewer timeout
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Skill Research auto-capture was still eligible on automatic/helper `agent_end` sessions, including cron, hook-scoped, and Active Memory helper session keys, so routine automation could queue Skill Workshop proposals from sessions that should not produce durable user-correction proposals.
- The harness keeps Skill Research auto-capture detached for fire-and-forget/channel-backed lifecycle paths, but direct local CLI runs now await auto-capture settlement so an eligible proposal is persisted before the command returns.
- Fix classification: root-cause fix at the active Skill Research auto-capture owner and agent lifecycle side-effect boundary.
- Root cause: the Skill Research lifecycle invariant was missing at the proposal-capture source; every successful `agent_end` could be treated as a durable instruction source, so automatic/helper session state reached Skill Workshop proposal extraction and writes.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the eligibility decision now runs inside `src/skills/research/autocapture.ts` before signal extraction or workshop writes, because that file is the source-of-truth boundary that turns session messages into persisted Skill Workshop proposals. Rejected automation/helper sessions never enter proposal creation, rather than timing out later, relying on a fallback, or masking the symptom downstream.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: the source-of-truth owner for proposal creation is Skill Research auto-capture; the agent harness only schedules lifecycle side effects; plugin hook settlement remains owned by the agent harness hook contract; the model-consumed transcript and raw session history are unchanged.
- What did NOT change: this PR does not change public config, schema, protocol, data format, storage layout, provider routing, model defaults, model-consumed transcript, plugin SDK contract, or user/manual capture eligibility. target.title: fix: web_search fails with transparent proxies when no proxy env vars are set
target.body:
## Summary

- **Fix `web_search` failing with `"fetch failed"` when using transparent proxies** (e.g. Proxifier + V2Ray) instead of `HTTP_PROXY`/`HTTPS_PROXY` env vars. In `TRUSTED_ENV_PROXY` mode (used by `web_search`), skip DNS pinning and custom undici dispatcher when no proxy env vars are configured, allowing the default fetch path to work with system-level transparent proxies.
- **Improve tool error diagnostics** by walking the `.cause` chain (up to 3 levels) in `describeToolExecutionError()`. Previously, undici's `TypeError: fetch failed` lost the actual network error (e.g. `ENOTFOUND`, `ECONNREFUSED`) buried in nested `.cause` properties, making debugging very difficult.

## Problem

`fetchWithSsrFGuard()` always created a custom undici `Agent` with a `connect: { lookup: pinnedLookup }` callback for SSRF protection. This custom dispatcher bypasses transparent proxy interception (Proxifier, V2Ray, etc.), causing `web_search` and `web_fetch` to fail with a generic `"fetch failed"` error — while model API calls (which use `globalThis.fetch` with the default dispatcher) work fine through the same proxy.

## Changes

### `src/infra/net/fetch-guard.ts`
- In `TRUSTED_ENV_PROXY` mode (web_search, operator-controlled URLs):
  - With proxy env vars set → route through `EnvHttpProxyAgent` (unchanged)
  - Without proxy env vars → skip DNS pinning entirely, leave dispatcher null, use default `globalThis.fetch` path (new behavior — enables transparent proxy interception)
- In `STRICT` mode (web_fetch, untrusted user URLs): behavior is unchanged — always pins DNS for SSRF protection

### `src/agents/pi-tool-definition-adapter.ts`
- Add `describeCauseChain()` helper that walks `err.cause` up to 3 levels
- Enhance `describeToolExecutionError()` to include cause chain in error messages (e.g. `"fetch failed (cause: connect ECONNREFUSED → connection refused)"`)

### Tests
- New test: `skips pinned dispatcher in trusted proxy mode when no proxy env vars are set` — verifies no dispatcher is attached and `lookupFn` is not called
- New tests: `includes error cause chain in tool error messages` and `includes deeply nested error cause chain (max 3 levels)` — verify cause chain is surfaced in tool error results
- All existing tests pass unchanged

## Security note

This change only affects `TRUSTED_ENV_PROXY` mode (used by `web_search` for operator-controlled API endpoints like Brave Search). `STRICT` mode (used by `web_fetch` for arbitrary user URLs) retains full SSRF DNS pinning protection.

target.title: fix(model-picker): preserve auth profile override when selecting a model
target.body:
## Summary

The native model pickers call `applyModelOverrideToSessionEntry()` (`src/sessions/model-overrides.ts`) with only a model `selection` and no `profileOverride`. In that case the helper takes its default-clear branch and **unconditionally deletes** `authProfileOverride`, `authProfileOverrideSource`, and `authProfileOverrideCompactionCount` unless the caller passes `preserveAuthProfileOverride: true`. The pickers don't, so picking a model silently destroys an auth profile override that was established by a *different* source (a CLI flag, a session-level override) — the user just sees "Model changed to X" with no warning.

Fixes #92244.

## Root cause

`applyModelOverrideToSessionEntry` has an opt-out preserve flag for exactly this case (see the existing conditional use in `src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts`). The pickers never set an auth profile (none of the three picker call sites pass `profileOverride`), so any `authProfileOverride` on the entry was owned by another source — yet the pickers omit `preserveAuthProfileOverride`, hitting the destructive branch at `src/sessions/model-overrides.ts` (the `else if (!params.preserveAuthProfileOverride)` block).

## Fix

Pass `preserveAuthProfileOverride: true` at the picker call sites so model selection no longer clobbers an auth profile override it doesn't own:

- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts` — the reported Telegram picker.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command-model-picker-apply.ts` — the Discord picker shares the identical omission and the same invariant, so it is fixed in the same change (sibling surface).

The core helper is unchanged — this is purely a caller-intent fix; the helper already supports and tests the flag.

### Sibling surfaces considered

- `extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.ts` calls the helper with `selectionSource: "auto"` for a config-driven response model, not a user picker. That is a different intent (automatic model application, not interactive selection), so it is intentionally left out of scope here rather than changed speculatively.

## Verification

- Behavior addressed: using a native model picker to switch models no longer deletes an `authProfileOverride` set by a non-picker source.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout — Vitest (Discord picker harness that drives the real submit-button flow through `applyModelOverrideToSessionEntry` and reads back the persisted session store), tsgo extension type lanes, oxfmt, oxlint.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.model-picker.test.ts` → 21 passed
  - Reverted only the Discord picker guard to current `main` and re-ran → the new regression fails: `AssertionError: expected undefined to be 'cli-profile'` (the override was silently deleted); restored the fix → 21 passed
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` and `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions` → exit 0
  - `oxfmt --check` / `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on touched files → clean
- Evidence after fix: with the fix the persisted entry keeps `authProfileOverride: "cli-profile"` and `authProfileOverrideSource: "user"` after the picker selects a different model; without it both are `undefined`.
- Observed result after fix: picker writes the new model override and leaves the unrelated auth profile override intact.
- What was not tested: a live Telegram round-trip (no test harness exists for the inline Telegram picker callback; the Telegram change is the identical one-line flag as the Discord fix, which is covered by the regression test, and the core helper's flag behavior is already unit-tested in `src/sessions/model-overrides.test.ts`).

## Tests

Added `extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.model-picker.test.ts` › "preserves an auth profile override set by another source when picking a model": pre-seeds a session entry with a `user`-sourced `authProfileOverride`, drives the picker to a different model, and asserts the override and its source survive. Fails on current `main`, passes with this change.

target.title: feat(memory): apply temporal decay to QMD search results
target.body:
## Problem

`memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` currently only applies in the builtin hybrid engine merge path (`mergeHybridResults` in `hybrid.ts`). When `memory.backend = "qmd"` — the backend the docs recommend for advanced recall — search results skip recency weighting entirely.

Real-world failure mode that motivated this: after a session compaction, searching for a keyword mentioned earlier the same day returned a months-old small file ranked above today's large daily memory file. The term appeared once in a ~44KB daily file, so its BM25 contribution was diluted (≈0.13) while a stale low-content file scored ≈0.42. With no recency weighting on the QMD path, the stale hit wins — exactly the situation temporal decay was added to fix for the builtin engine.

## Change

- Wire the existing `temporal-decay` module into `QmdMemoryManager.search()`: after doc resolution and source filtering, decay the combined ranking score by document age and re-rank before source diversification.
- Resolve the decay config through `resolveMemorySearchConfig(cfg, agentId)` so QMD honors the exact same `memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` knob as the builtin engine (no new config surface).
- Include the resolved decay config in the QMD manager cache identity key so config changes rebuild the manager.
- Docs: note in `reference/memory-config.md` that temporal decay now applies to both backends.

## Behavior

- **Default off** — `temporalDecay.enabled` still defaults to `false`; no behavior change unless opted in.
- Evergreen semantics match the builtin engine: `MEMORY.md` and non-dated `memory/` files never decay; dated files parse from filename, other files fall back to mtime.
- When disabled, the QMD result pipeline is untouched (no extra fs calls).

## Tests

- New test in `qmd-manager.test.ts`: with decay enabled (30-day half-life), a 60-day-old dated memory file with raw score 0.9 ranks below a same-day file with raw score 0.6 (decayed ≈0.225 vs ≈0.6).
- Full runs of `qmd-manager.test.ts`, `search-manager.test.ts`, `temporal-decay.test.ts`, `hybrid.test.ts`: 172 passed.
- `pnpm tsgo` clean; oxlint clean on touched files.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)


---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** With `memorySearch.query.hybrid.temporalDecay` enabled, QMD search results re-rank by document age so a fresh dated memory file outranks a stale, keyword-dense file that wins on raw BM25/rerank score; with decay disabled, ranking is byte-identical to current behavior.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS (arm64), Node v25.6.1, real `qmd` binary on PATH with an isolated QMD home (`XDG_CONFIG_HOME`/`XDG_CACHE_HOME`/`QMD_CONFIG_DIR` pointed at a scratch dir), real workspace with two dated memory files: `memory/2026-04-11.md` (60 days old, query term 4x) and `memory/2026-06-10.md` (today, single mention). This branch's `temporal-decay.ts` executed via tsx against the live qmd scores.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `qmd collection add "$WS" --name demo --mask "**/*.md"` && `qmd update` (Indexed: 2 new) && `qmd query "tachyon relay calibration" --json -n 10 -c demo`, then fed the captured results through this branch's `applyTemporalDecayToHybridResults({enabled: true, halfLifeDays: 3, workspaceDir: $WS})` and re-sorted — the exact transform `QmdMemoryManager.search()` applies in this PR.
- **Evidence after fix:** copied live terminal output below.

```
=== qmd query ranking — decay DISABLED (current QMD behavior) ===
  0.9300  memory/2026-04-11.md   # 60 days old, keyword-dense
  0.5600  memory/2026-06-10.md   # today, single mention

=== same query — decay ENABLED (this PR, halfLifeDays=3) ===
  0.4465  memory/2026-06-10.md   # today -> now ranked #1
  0.0000  memory/2026-04-11.md   # 60d old -> decayed below

Ranking flipped so today's note wins: YES
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Without decay the stale file wins 0.93 vs 0.56 (the dilution problem this PR fixes). With decay enabled, today's note keeps ~0.79 of its score (age < 1 day) while the 60-day file decays by `2^(-60/3) ≈ 1e-6` to ~0.0000 — the ranking flips, matching the builtin hybrid engine's decay semantics on the same inputs.
- **What was not tested:** mcporter transport path and multi-collection-group searches in a live gateway session (the same decay transform applies after result assembly; both covered by unit tests), Windows hosts, and the `halfLifeDays: 30` default in a live run (covered by the 60-day-fixture unit test).

Supplemental: `pnpm tsgo` clean; oxlint clean; `qmd-manager` / `search-manager` / `temporal-decay` / `hybrid` suites pass, including new tests for decay re-rank, post-decay `minScore` floor, and the x4/cap-50 candidate pool.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Channel echo / session pinning (pin-from-here) with native Telegram rendering A session can mirror its turns to other threads that are bound to the **same** session, so a long run you started in one place can be followed from another — and the mirror renders **natively** in the destination channel (live draft stream, not a flat `[echo]` copy). ### What changed since the earlier revision (maintainer feedback) The earlier revision let a session pin **arbitrary** `{channel, to, threadId}` echo targets. The maintainer's concern was that continuous mirroring to arbitrary chat ids is a heavy primitive and lacks an authorization story for streaming conversation content to any chat id. This revision reshapes the opt-in surface to **pin-from-here**, which removes that surface entirely: - A thread opts **itself** in with the in-chat command **`/pin on`** (alias `/mirror`). It can only mirror the session it is **already bound to** — there is no arbitrary-target argument — so a pin can never cross a session boundary. Every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that exact session. **Zero new authorization surface.** - The operator add paths (`sessions echo` CLI + `sessions.echo` RPC) now **reject any target that is not a verified participant** of the session (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), with guidance to use `/pin` from the target thread instead. The "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path is gone. - Self-exclusion is correct by construction: `/pin` captures the thread's own origin identity (the exact fields the fan-out uses as a turn's origin), so when this thread drives a turn it is excluded from its own fan-out; when a sibling thread of the same session drives, this thread receives the mirror. `last*` (the session's persistent external delivery route) is unchanged — a transient webchat/cron poke still must not hijack an external session's route (session-delivery.ts, #47745). ### Native rendering for every origin The native streaming renderer replays the origin run's agent-event bus into each pinned channel's own compositor (one agent run, N native renders), so the mirror carries the destination channel's real rendering rather than a flat final. **The mirror rides the destination thread's own streaming config** — it renders the turn as if it had happened natively in that thread, not a stripped subset. The mirror resolver already replays the origin run's full event stream (`assistant`/`item`/`tool`/`reasoning`/`plan`/`command`/`patch`) into the target's `GetReplyOptions`, but the Telegram echo renderer previously implemented only `onPartialReply`, so the verbose tool-progress lane was silently dropped regardless of how the destination was configured. The renderer now honors the same callbacks the native compositor honors, gated on the destination account's own config and reusing the shared `buildChannelProgressDraftLineForEntry` / `formatChannelProgressDraftText` / `mergeChannelProgressDraftLine` primitives: in `progress` stream mode with `preview.toolProgress`, the tool-progress lane renders (held for the turn, collapsed to the answer at finalize — matching native progress rendering); in `partial` mode the answer streams alone, as a native group reply would. No toggle — the mirror rides whatever that channel would do. This revision also fixes a latent bug so the native path engages for **non- channel-origin** turns (webchat `chat.send`, cron/heartbeat): the fan-out derived its origin from the session's `last*`, which those turns deliberately do not claim — so the origin defaulted to the previously-active channel and, when that channel was itself the pinned target, the fan-out **self-excluded** it and fell back to the flat post-hoc mirror. It now derives the fan-out origin from the turn's **actual** origin (`OriginatingChannel/To`), falling back to `last*`, so a webchat trigger renders natively in the pinned channel. ### New in this revision (4 commits on top of the existing channel-echo work) - `feat(reply): add /pin (pin-from-here) in-chat command` — `commands-pin.ts` + registration; stores the caller's origin identity into the bound session's echo targets. Unit tests incl. an end-to-end check chaining `/pin` through the real `resolveEchoTargets` fan-out (sibling included, self excluded). - `feat(echo): reject arbitrary-target echo adds; require a session participant` — `targetMatchesSessionParticipant` in `echo.ts`, wired into both operator add paths. Unit tests for accept/reject/fail-closed. - `fix(echo): native streaming fan-out uses the turn's actual origin, not stale last*` — `agent-runner-execution.ts`. - `feat(telegram): echo renderer rides the destination's streaming config` — `extensions/telegram/src/echo-renderer.ts` (+ `echo-renderer-register.ts` threading the account config/stream mode). Wires the tool-progress lane (`onToolStart`/`onItemEvent`/`onPlanUpdate`/`onCommandOutput`/`onPatchSummary`/ `onApprovalEvent`) the resolver already drives but the renderer ignored; fully contained to the Telegram extension (no core change). New unit tests assert the lane is wired in `progress` mode and absent in `partial` mode. The existing channel-echo infrastructure (native streaming fan-out, mirror resolver, owner-bound `registerEchoRendererFactory`, the types-only `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath) is otherwise unchanged. ### Tests + CI (current head `0388fad1115`) - `pnpm tsgo:core` + `tsgo:extensions`: 0 errors on the changed surface. `oxlint` on the changed files: 0 warnings / 0 errors. - Unit tests for the changed surface pass, including the **152-test `agent-runner-execution.test`** suite the origin fix touches, plus `commands-pin.test`, `echo.test`, `chat.send-user-echo.test`, and the echo suite (`echo-renderer.test` / `echo-renderer-register.test` / `echo-streaming.test`, 48 tests) — the new echo-renderer tests assert the tool-progress lane is wired in `progress` mode and absent in `partial` mode. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow with a local patched crabbox: a real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch (`OpenClaw 2026.5.31 d6c154c`, global-installed) + Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, run locally — driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with `/verbose on` and an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread and webchat are bound to one session; the thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt, and the pinned thread receives that webchat-origin turn — including the real tool result — rendered natively. Captured sequence (real Telegram Desktop, see image/video below): 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Run the shell command: date -u — use your exec tool, then tell me the UTC time…` — the webchat-origin, tool-using prompt 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The current UTC time is 14:35:12 on Thursday, June 11, 2026."** — i.e. a real `date -u` exec, run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread. A real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` turn (with `/verbose on` and an exec `date -u` tool call) is triggered from webchat and mirrored natively into the pinned Telegram thread — autoplay GIF (left, trimmed to the reply landing) + final frame (right):
Native-path engagement on the same build was confirmed by instrumenting the fan-out: for the webchat-origin turn `resolveEchoTargets` returns the Telegram target (origin = webchat, not self-excluded), the factory is found, and the renderer is **created** (native). Gateway log for the filmed run: `session turn created … channel=webchat` → the real claude turn ran the exec tool (`outcome=completed`) and the result rendered in the pinned thread. Session store after `/pin`: `echoTargets: [{"channel":"telegram","to":"telegram:-1003900553563","threadId":1,"label":"pinned"}]`. #### Riding the destination config — the tool-progress lane renders in the mirror With the destination thread in `progress` stream mode (`/verbose on`, `preview.toolProgress`), the mirror renders the **verbose tool-progress lane**, not just the answer. A real `claude-cli` turn triggered from webchat runs an exec tool (`sleep 4; hostname`) — chosen so the result is not derivable from context, proving a genuine tool call — and the pinned Telegram thread shows the **`🛠️ Bash`** progress lane while the tool runs, then collapses to the answer **`The hostname is crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`** (the real box hostname). Autoplay GIF of the full `prompt → 🛠️ Bash lane → answer` sequence (left) and the tool-lane frame (right):
**Proof environment:** single-node local gateway, one Telegram backend, isolated state dir; real `claude-cli` model with exec tools enabled. Sensitive chat ids are the test supergroup, not production. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). Implementation, tests, and the local behavior-proof capture were AI-assisted; framing and the proof are the author's.

target.title: feat(gateway): wire read coding tool into HTTP /tools/invoke (narrow)
target.body:
Related to #66561 (orthogonal — direct-invoke read benefits the embedded run-budget cascade but does not fix the underlying SSE timeout it describes; that issue should remain open). narrow first landing of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Wire the `read` coding tool into the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver) so it is reachable for deterministic automation — CI/preflight checks, lint pipelines, browser capture flows — without an LLM round-trip.

**This PR is the redesigned narrow read-only landing.** The prior 10-commit history was reset to a single fresh commit on top of current upstream `7c1a83ff2e`, restructured to satisfy ClawSweeper's [P1] from the prior review: **dual-key gating** so the existing `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` config knob does not silently grant host-FS read on upgrade.

## Dual-key gating (addresses ClawSweeper [P1])

To enable `read` on the direct-invoke surface, operators must set BOTH:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` | **NEW.** Distinct surface-specific opt-in. Without this, `read` is not materialized into the candidate set even if `tools.allow` includes it. | `false` (omitted) |
| `gateway.tools.allow: ["read"]` | Lifts `read` from `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` (now extended with `"read"`). Existing knob; keeps operator-acceptance step visible in the standard policy surface. | not present |

**Either key alone leaves `read` unreachable.** Old configs that already contain `allow: ["read"]` for unrelated reasons (e.g. an MCP/agent surface where `read` was always available) stay inert at the direct-invoke layer — no silent upgrade-time expansion.

| `tools.allow` includes `"read"` | `directInvoke.hostFsRead` | `read` reachable on direct-invoke |
|---|---|---|
| no  | no   | ❌ |
| yes | no   | ❌ (tool not materialized) |
| no  | yes  | ❌ (filtered by HTTP deny) |
| yes | yes  | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Read-only.** Mutating coding primitives (`write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` / `exec` / `process`) are NOT exposed by this PR.
- **Both surfaces.** Gating applies to HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND the SDK-facing JSON-RPC `tools.invoke` (they share `resolveGatewayScopedTools`).
- **MCP loopback unaffected.** `surface === "loopback"` is not gated.
- **Hook-bypass safe.** Uses new `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export (module-private Symbol marker, not reachable from typed SDK params) so HTTP-surface tools aren't double-wrapped — `handleToolsInvokeHttp` already runs `runBeforeToolCallHook` itself before dispatch.

## Files changed

- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — `SKIP_BEFORE_TOOL_CALL_HOOK` symbol + `createOpenClawCodingToolsRaw` export
- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — `GatewayToolsConfig.directInvoke: GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` field (new type)
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — `"read"` added to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — dual-key gated materialization of `read` into candidate set
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_read_allow` finding (only fires when BOTH gates set; silent for either alone since `read` is unreachable there)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — 5 new test cases covering all 4 gate combinations + vanilla
- `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` — operator-facing dual-key opt-in docs with config example + truth table

## Code-level test coverage

`src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` (new `describe` block):

- ✅ fires (warn) when both keys set on loopback bind
- ✅ escalates to critical when bind is lan
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `allow: ["read"]` (legacy shape)
- ✅ does NOT fire when only `hostFsRead: true`
- ✅ does NOT fire on vanilla config

## Why a single fresh commit (not rebase of prior 10)

The original PR's 10 commits implemented an OLD design (single-key `allow: ["read"]` as the opt-in). The bot's P1 — "Require a fresh opt-in before enabling host reads" — needed a fundamentally different shape, not patches on top. Plus the prior commits referenced `src/agents/pi-tools.ts` which has been renamed to `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` upstream. Cleaner to reset to upstream/main and write the redesign as one commit than rebase-then-patch.

Backup of prior PR history retained at `backup/85664-pre-rebase-20260603-onto-7c1a83ff2e` on the public fork.

---

## Real behavior proof

> **Note (head `b264b15a7a`):** The live proof below ran at `0a0cafdef7`. The current head adds one docs-formatting-only commit (`oxfmt` table re-alignment of `audit-checks.md`, whitespace/dash padding) with **zero runtime code change**, so the proof remains valid for the current head.

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Expose the `read` coding tool over Gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the resolver) for deterministic automation, gated behind a NEW distinct `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead: true` opt-in. Addresses ClawSweeper [P1]: a single-key `allow: ["read"]` legacy config must NOT silently grant host-FS read on upgrade.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `git checkout 0a0cafdef7 && docker run --name oc-85664-proof -v $PWD:/work -v oc_proof_nm:/work/node_modules --platform linux/arm64 -e NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=12288 node:22-bookworm-slim bash /work/.build-and-proof-85664.sh` — exit 0.

**Real environment tested:** built fresh from head `0a0cafdef7d0a61ae87e6836272beb7f199aefdf` inside `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (pnpm v11.2.2, linux-arm64 packages in named volume). Gateway started per case via `openclaw gateway run`; each case dispatched via HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK `GatewayClientTransport` WebSocket `oc.tools.invoke(...)`.
Unit tests (Docker, linux/arm64): 115 total — 10/10 (audit-gateway-tools-http) + 105/105 (tools-invoke-http). Full build + SDK build: all phases completed.

**Evidence after fix (terminal capture, head `0a0cafdef7`):** from `docker run node:22-bookworm-slim` (linux/arm64):

```
=== HEAD: 0a0cafdef7d0a61ae87e6836272beb7f199aefdf ===

### TESTS (audit-gateway-tools-http) ###
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       10 passed (10)

### TESTS (tools-invoke-http) ###
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       105 passed (105)

### BUILD ###
[build-all] plugins:assets:build done in 407ms
[build-all] tsdown done in 242.8s
BUILD OK

### SDK BUILD ###
dist/index.mjs  30.52 kB
SDK BUILD OK

### LIVE PROOF ###

--- R-DENIED-no-opt-in ---
  [cfg] allow=["read"] hfsR=false
  [gw] ready
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}
  [SDK] ok=false {"ok":false,"toolName":"read","error":{"code":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}

--- R-ENABLED ---
  [cfg] allow=["read"] hfsR=true
  [gw] ready
  [HTTP] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"PROOF READ SOURCE at 0a0cafdef7d0a61ae87e6836272beb7f199aefdf\n"}]}}
  [SDK] ok=true {"ok":true,"toolName":"read","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"PROOF READ SOURCE at 0a0cafdef7d0a61ae87e6836272beb7f199aefdf\n"}]},"source":"core"}

--- R-DENIED-no-allow ---
  [cfg] allow=[] hfsR=true
  [gw] ready
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}
  [SDK] ok=false {"ok":false,"toolName":"read","error":{"code":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: read"}}

--- WRITE-still-denied ---
  [cfg] allow=["write"] hfsR=true
  [gw] ready
  [HTTP] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  [SDK] ok=false {"ok":false,"toolName":"write","error":{"code":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}

=== FINAL FILE STATES ===

```

**Observed result after fix (head `0a0cafdef7`):** All 8 dispatches (4 cases × HTTP+SDK) from `docker run node:22-bookworm-slim` behave identically. R-DENIED-no-opt-in: `allow=["read"]` alone → 404 on both surfaces (P1 fix: legacy config stays inert). R-ENABLED: both keys set → HTTP 200 with actual sentinel file content, SDK ok=true with same content. R-DENIED-no-allow: `hostFsRead=true` alone → 404 (proves both keys required). WRITE-still-denied: `allow=["write"]` with `hostFsRead=true` → 404 for `write` (read-only scope confirmed).

**What was not tested:** Write/edit/exec/process are out of scope for this read-only PR (covered by #63919 for write/edit). `apply_patch` is in deny list, not yet factory-produced.

## Docs discoverability

Addresses ClawSweeper's [P2]: "Document the host-FS read opt-in everywhere" (`src/config/zod-schema.ts:1037-1040`).

- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — FIELD_HELP entries for `gateway.tools.directInvoke` and `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsRead`.
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` — paragraph describing the dual-key gating + audit warning hookup.
- `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — catalog row for `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_read_allow` with severity, criteria, and config-key references.

So operators using config help/lookup or security-audit docs can now discover this security-sensitive opt-in and warning.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: fix(gateway): fail fast on missing local probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fail-fast in `openclaw gateway status` for the local loopback case: when `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials but neither the persisted token nor an interactive credential can be resolved, the status command synthesises a failed probe with a clear `failureReason` instead of attempting an anonymous WebSocket connection that surfaces as a misleading `Connect: failed`.

Explicit `--url`/`--rpc-url` targets are intentionally exempt: a user pointing at a remote, forwarded, or open gateway should not be blocked by missing local-config credentials.

## Details

- `resolveAuthForTarget` resolves through the probe-surface helper. When the result has neither token nor password AND the target is the known local loopback AND `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials, it falls back to the interactive-surface helper and propagates the failure reason.
- The fail-fast is scoped to `surface === "local" && target.kind === "localLoopback"`. Targets with `kind === "explicit"` (i.e. `--url`/`--rpc-url`) skip the check.
- Tests cover both the local fail-fast path and the explicit-target carve-out, and isolate ambient `gateway.auth.token` env in the probe-auth test to avoid cross-test contamination.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: before this patch, `openclaw gateway status` against a local config with `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` but an unresolvable token (e.g. `secrets.token.env` points at a non-existent env var) would still try an anonymous WebSocket connection and surface `Connect: failed` to the operator. The operator-actionable diagnosis ("missing required local auth") was buried under a misleading network error. After this patch, the probe is short-circuited with a `failureReason` that names the auth shape.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/wt-68280` (rebased onto `upstream/main` at `8e1b0e61b0`), run against an isolated test config at `/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json` so the live mac-mini gateway and its real auth are not touched. The test config sets `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` and `gateway.secrets.token.env` to an env var that is explicitly unset in the shell.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  unset OPENCLAW_PROBE_NONEXISTENT_TEST_VAR
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `node dist/index.js` runtime built from this PR head. The first scenario is the local-loopback fail-fast; the second is the explicit-`--url` carve-out.

  ```
  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Missing gateway auth token.",
    "kind": null
  }

  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  {
    "rpc": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18999",
      "kind": "connect"
    },
    "probeUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18999"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: in the local-loopback scenario, `rpc.kind` is `null` (no probe attempt was made) and `rpc.error` names the actionable auth diagnostic instead of a network error. In the explicit-URL scenario, `rpc.kind` is `"connect"` (the probe DID run) and the error is a real network-level `ECONNREFUSED` against the URL the operator supplied. The design boundary holds: missing local SecretRef diagnostics do not leak into the explicit-URL probe contract.

- **What was not tested**: the explicit-URL daemon `--rpc-url` flag was not exercised end-to-end in this proof. The carve-out is structural (`target.kind !== "explicit"` is the only gate) and is covered by the regression tests added in `c6c648a9cd` and `8e1b0e61b0`.

target.title: Fix stale cron catch-up after schedule updates
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91944.

Cron restart catch-up can infer a missed cron slot from `lastRunAtMs` even when `nextRunAtMs` already points to the future. That catch-up path is valid for ordinary restart recovery, but after `cron.update` changes scheduling inputs it can replay an old slot that predates the new schedule's activation.

This change records when schedule/enabled inputs are updated and makes startup catch-up skip inferred cron slots older than that activation time. The focused regression covers the reported monthly schedule shape, an isolated `agentTurn`, and the 120s startup deferral path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: a cron job updated from a day-10 monthly schedule to a day-11 monthly schedule no longer gets treated as a missed day-11 slot from the previous month and deferred to `gateway startup + 120s` on restart.

Real environment tested: local macOS source checkout at `26b5b5efb9` with Node 24.15.0 and pnpm 11.2.2. The runtime proof used an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and OpenClaw's real SQLite cron store path, then imported the source `CronService`, `saveCronStore`, and `loadCronStore` modules directly from this PR head.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module` from the repository root with a stdin script that seeded the SQLite-backed cron store, called `CronService.update("monthly-agent-report", { schedule: { kind: "cron", expr: "18 15 11 * *", tz: "UTC" } })` at `2026-06-09T22:30:00.000Z`, then started a fresh `CronService` at `2026-06-10T20:33:00.000Z` with `startupDeferredMissedAgentJobDelayMs: 120000`.

target.title: Revamp command authorization candidates
target.body:
## Summary

- replace the ad hoc POSIX authorization path with a Tree-sitter-backed command candidate planner built on `explainShellCommand`
- route shell/argv allowlist checks and allow-always persistence through planned candidates, including static shell-wrapper payloads
- convert exec control-shell policy to a generic static command matcher over normalized argv/options/operands
- keep approval storage compatible while preserving safer fallback behavior for dynamic wrappers, path-scoped payloads, and pipe-to-shell cases
- keep the UI showing the actual command while honoring `allowedDecisions`

## Decisions and rationale

- POSIX shell authorization now comes from one planned command model: `planShellAuthorization()` uses the Tree-sitter-backed `explainShellCommand()` path, and the plan is carried into allowlist evaluation, allow-always persistence, and shell rewrite. The old POSIX splitter/render fallback is no longer an alternate source of candidates.
- Inline shell candidates are generated once during policy evaluation and then reused. The node-host safe-bin rewrite no longer re-runs `evaluateSystemRunAllowlist()` on the rendered command; it verifies that the plan still belongs to the original shell payload and renders from that plan.
- Exec control-shell policy now follows `Tree-sitter static extraction -> normalized command invocation -> hard-coded policy rule`.
- `/approve` handling is removed from exec control-shell policy. `/approve` is a control/chat action, not an executable policy concept, and exec should not carry a special deny list for old agent behavior. The old command-analysis carrier fixture block that used `/approve` has been deleted instead of renamed to another binary.
- Safe bins are treated as narrow, argument-profiled execution shortcuts, not blanket shell trust. Static, parsed safe-bin candidates may be rewritten to resolved trusted paths. Dynamic shells, command substitution, line continuations, path-scoped wrapper payloads, and pipe-to-shell cases remain approval-gated unless the whole relevant command graph is allowed.
- Static reads under `.ssh` / `~/.ssh` through basic read commands are now approval-required hard-coded control policy. Dynamic paths are not resolved by this matcher; they remain governed by the normal exec approval flow.
- Background `&` is preserved as shell topology. The planner models it as a background relationship and the renderer emits `&` when rebuilding commands, instead of failing closed or collapsing it into a sequence separator.
- `deny` still wins when deny-mode policy is active. The authorization planner can explain candidates, but runtime policy decides whether execution is allowed.

## Behavior changes

- Static POSIX shell wrappers like `sh -c 'git status'` can now be authorized by the inner `git` candidate when the payload is static and safely parsed.
- Dynamic wrappers like `sh -c '$CMD'`, command substitution, line continuations, and path-scoped wrapper payloads stay approval-gated instead of becoming reusable inner approvals.
- `curl URL | sh` / `curl URL | bash` behavior is preserved: both sides must be allowed for auto-run; allow-always persists `curl` only; persisted `curl` alone does not authorize the shell side on rerun.
- Mixed allowlist + safe-bin shell-wrapper payloads are rebuilt before execution from the existing authorization plan so the executed wrapper uses trusted resolved paths.
- Exec control-shell policy denies OpenClaw channel login commands and requires approval for security audit suppression mutation through the same normalized command matcher.
- Static `cat` / `less` / `more` / `head` / `tail` reads under `.ssh` or `~/.ssh` now require explicit approval.
- `ask=always` approval requests no longer offer an unusable Always Allow action in the Control UI.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: Candidate-based command authorization for POSIX shell and non-Windows argv execution, including shell-wrapper unwrapping, dynamic fallback, pipe-to-shell behavior, allow-always persistence, node-host execution, control-shell dangerous command policy, and UI approval decisions.

Real environment tested: Local macOS focused tests; local OpenClaw Control UI against the candidate worktree; Blacksmith Testbox through Crabbox.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-authorization-plan.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-allow-always.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-analysis.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-safe-bins.test.ts src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-policy.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts
git diff --check
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider blacksmith-testbox --blacksmith-org openclaw --blacksmith-workflow .github/workflows/ci-check-testbox.yml --blacksmith-job check --blacksmith-ref main --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json -- CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=6 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 OPENCLAW_TESTBOX=1 OPENCLAW_TESTBOX_REMOTE_RUN=1 pnpm check:changed
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/node-host/invoke-system-run-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-approvals-allow-always.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-plan.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run-allowlist.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/command-analysis/risks.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/command-explainer/extract.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-plan.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-control-shell-policy.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run-allowlist.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.script-preflight.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/command-explainer/extract.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-analysis.test.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-allow-always.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run-allowlist.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Focused local proof passed 15 files / 465 tests. `git diff --check` passed. Testbox-through-Crabbox `tbx_01ks1f6v0x35t56nymmxbaw4a1` / GitHub Actions run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26135215597 completed `pnpm check:changed` with exit 0. Follow-up cleanup proof passed 2 files / 20 tests, then 6 files / 119 tests, then control-shell/preflight/carrier focused proof passed 4 files / 148 tests with 8 skipped. After removing the repo doc and deleting the old carrier fixture block, focused proof passed 4 files / 147 tests with 8 skipped. After the reduction pass, focused proof passed 7 files / 212 tests with 8 skipped, and `git diff --check` passed. After the test-rubbish audit cleanup, focused proof passed 5 files / 177 tests, `git diff --check` passed, and the changed-test diff is now +1804 / -944 / net +860.

Observed result after fix: Static shell wrappers can auto-run from trusted inner candidates; dynamic/path-scoped wrapper payloads still prompt; `curl | sh` requires both sides; allow-always saved `/usr/bin/git` for a live `sh -c 'git status --short'` run and the rerun produced no second approval request. Static `.ssh` reads through basic read commands now require approval.

What was not tested: Full release validation was not run. Windows behavior remains on the legacy fallback path. The local UI proof used `--no-channels`, so Slack/Discord listeners were not part of the manual proof.

## UI proof notes

- Started local OpenClaw from this worktree with `openclaw-local-test --repo ... --no-open --no-channels --ttl 2h`.
- The installed local-test helper was machine-locally adjusted to stop writing stale `tools.exec.mode = "auto"`; that was not a repo change.
- The Control UI displayed the actual command `sh -c 'git status --short'`, including Host, Agent, Session, CWD, Resolved, Security, and Ask fields.
- Clicking Always Allow resolved the prompt.
- A live chat/tool run then created a saved `/usr/bin/git` allow-always entry, rerunning the same prompt produced no second `exec.approval.requested` event within 30 seconds, and the temporary `/usr/bin/git` test entry was removed from `~/.openclaw/exec-approvals.json` afterward.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): guard against missing agentIds in public artifact clone and sort (fixes #92207)
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: All `wiki_*` tools throw `artifact.agentIds is not iterable` because `listMemoryHostPublicArtifacts()` may return artifacts with `agentIds` undefined for some workspaces.
- **Root Cause**: `cloneMemoryPublicArtifact` spreads `artifact.agentIds` into `[...artifact.agentIds]`, crashing on undefined. The `.toSorted()` comparator calls `.join()` on potentially undefined `agentIds`.
- **Fix**: Guard clone with `Array.isArray` check defaulting to `[]`. Guard sort comparator with `(agentIds ?? [])` and `(contentType ?? "")` fallbacks.
- **What changed**: `src/plugins/memory-state.ts` — 2 guard sites (+6/-3 lines)
- **What did NOT change**: `src/plugin-sdk/memory-host-core.ts` (agentIds always set from params there); no schema/type changes

### Reproduction

1. Configure memory wiki with a workspace lacking agent ID associations
2. Run any `wiki_*` tool
3. **Before**: `artifact.agentIds is not iterable`
4. **After**: Wiki tools operate with missing agentIds treated as empty

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed (92207)**: `wiki_*` tools crash with `artifact.agentIds is not iterable` when public artifacts lack agent ID associations. After fix, missing agentIds are guarded at both clone and sort sites.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node 22 — OpenClaw test harness exercising memory state plugin registry

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/memory-state.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix**:
```text
 RUN  v4.1.7 /home/0668001395/openclawProject1

 src/plugins/memory-state.test.ts (14 tests) 331ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

**Observed result after fix**: The memory state test suite covering capability registration, prompt building, corpus supplements, and public artifact listing all pass. cloneMemoryPublicArtifact safely handles artifacts with missing agentIds; the sort comparator no longer dereferences undefined fields.

**What was not tested**: A live memory wiki workspace with a real public artifact provider returning artifacts with missing agentIds was not driven.

**Repro confirmation**: The existing memory-state tests cover cloneMemoryPublicArtifact and listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts. The guarded sites match exactly the locations identified in the issue's patch.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: Empty `agentIds: []` in cloned artifacts could change downstream behavior.
  **Mitigation**: `agentIds` is typed as `string[]`; empty array is a valid value and downstream `.join()` already handles it.
- **Risk**: `contentType` undefined could cause sort ordering changes.
  **Mitigation**: Empty string is the standard default for localeCompare; sort order is now stable rather than crashing.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / wiki plugins

### Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/memory-state.test.ts`

N/A (initial submission)

target.title: fix(webchat): surface Codex commentary progress
target.body:
## Summary
- project Codex app-server `item/preamble` progress events into the WebChat transient chat stream
- tag progress-only streams so final/aborted materialization does not persist commentary as assistant history
- clarify Telegram docs: tool progress is default, Codex preamble/commentary requires `streaming.progress.commentary`

## Trace
- PR #89505 is merged into the latest release, but it only covered the ACP parent-stream path.
- The default Codex app-server harness already emits commentary as `stream: "item"`, `kind: "preamble"` progress events.
- Telegram on current main already routes those preamble events through `createChannelProgressDraftCompositor`, gated by `streaming.progress.commentary`.
- WebChat was still ignoring `item/preamble` agent events, so runs could appear silent until final/tool output.

## Tests
- `pnpm vitest run ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: WebChat now surfaces native Codex app-server `stream: "item"`, `kind: "preamble"` commentary progress as transient conversation text while a run is active, without storing that progress as assistant history after the run finalizes.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-native-commentary-progress` on macOS, using Node with `tsx` against the actual WebChat event handlers in `ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts` and `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran a Node replay of the actual WebChat handlers with a Codex app-server `item/preamble` event containing `progressText: "Checking the app-server stream"`, then sent the run `final` event.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output copied from the after-patch run:

```json
{
  "afterPreamble": {
    "chatStream": "Checking the app-server stream",
    "chatStreamKind": "progress",
    "chatStreamStartedAt": 1760000000000,
    "chatMessages": []
  },
  "finalResult": "final",
  "afterFinal": {
    "chatRunId": null,
    "chatStream": null,
    "chatStreamKind": null,
    "chatMessages": []
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The preamble progress text appears in the transient `chatStream` with `chatStreamKind: "progress"` during the run, and after the final event the transient stream is cleared while `chatMessages` remains empty, so commentary progress is not persisted as assistant content.
- **What was not tested**: No Telegram bot was launched in this proof run; Telegram behavior is covered by the existing config-gated compositor tests and requires `channels.telegram.streaming.progress.commentary=true` in runtime config.
